relevance_reasoning: The primary dataset is microsatellite genotypes (genetic_analysis) explicitly collected by the authors and deposited to Dryad, but it covers only two consecutive years (2006–2007) and no explicit spatial extent in km² is provided. Because the study species is listed as endangered (threatened_species = true), the relevance is upgraded from low temporal/spatial coverage to moderate for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The paper provides a primary dataset of relative abundance (captures per 100 trap‑nights) for two species collected in Sept–Oct 2008 with georeferenced trap locations, so data type is high-value (abundance) but temporal coverage is a short single survey and spatial extent is not quantified. Given the short temporal extent and limited species scope, overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is assessed as Low (L).

Long-term, high-resolution GPS and VHF telemetry across 1999–2000 and 2004–2011 (repeated locations per individual, ~460,000 locations) covering ~7,250 km² in Quebec and focused on woodland caribou (a federally listed threatened taxon). These attributes (time series, substantial spatial coverage within Quebec, and threatened species focus) make the dataset highly relevant for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Telemetry GPS locations (sampled points) and derived seasonal home ranges cover a multi-year period (2004–2011) across a 7,250 km² study area in Quebec, and the focal taxon is a nationally listed threatened species (woodland caribou). These features give high relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring (multiyear, moderate spatial extent, threatened focal species).

The primary dataset is microsatellite genetic data (genetic_analysis) collected across southern Quebec and deposited to Dryad (2011–2014), providing time-series genetic sampling (4 years) at repeated sites. Temporal extent (4 years = moderate) and limited spatial extent (~2080 km² = low) yield an overall moderate relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring, despite the high value of genetic data for vector surveillance.

Primary dataset consists of microsatellite genotypes (18 loci) collected by the authors from sugar maple across 23 populations in Québec (samples collected in 2008–2009) with mapped site locations and site identifiers. This is a genetic-analysis dataset (highly relevant EBV type), sampled over a large spatial extent within Quebec, so overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is high.

Primary dataset comprises microsatellite genotypes (genetic analysis) from 98 unique Canis samples collected across southern Ontario and Gatineau, QC (sample-level locations and a sampling map are provided) spanning 2003–2014. The data include identified Eastern Wolves (a COSEWIC-listed threatened taxon) and effective population size estimates directly relevant to Quebec biodiversity monitoring and recovery planning.

High relevance: the study provides abundance (percent cover) data for four Ericaceae aggregated from 15,339 ecological plots into 1,422 ecological districts covering 535,355 km2 of Quebec (1986–2000), producing spatial maps and regionalizations useful for provincial biodiversity monitoring and forest management.

High relevance: the authors collected georeferenced GPS time-series of 96 caribou (2007–2014) across >630,000 km² of northern Québec and linked movements to ice-phenology, providing directly applicable data for Quebec biodiversity monitoring and management.

Primary dataset contains microsatellite genotypes and reproductive-success (offspring counts) for Atlantic salmon collected in the Malbaie River (Quebec) across multiple years (2002–2005), making it directly relevant for regional biodiversity monitoring though spatial extent is limited to a single river.

Contains primary genetic (SNP) data and trait measurements for native Populus at a Quebec study site (BPSF), making it directly useful for local biodiversity monitoring; however, the study is spatially small (≈1.36 km²) and temporally short (2009–2011), so overall relevance is moderate.

Primary data are species-level abundance counts for small mammals and beetles and stand-structure measurements (abundance + ecosystem structure). Temporal coverage is two sampling years (2007 and 2011, 5 years span) and spatial extent is reported as site-level sampling across the Côte-Nord (Quebec) study area but an overall km2 extent is not stated. Multispecies sampling (many beetle species) and the explicit Quebec (Côte-Nord) location increase relevance to Quebec biodiversity monitoring, yielding a moderate relevance score.

Primary dataset contains 7 years (2005–2011) of tree swallow (Tachycineta bicolor) monitoring in southeastern Quebec (~10,200 km²) including individual trait measures (body mass, wing/tarsus), brood counts and nest-box occupancy. Data type (abundance + traits) is highly relevant but temporal (7 yr) and spatial (~10,200 km²) extents are moderate, so overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is moderate.

Moderately relevant: the study provides a 10-year, site-level time series (2004–2013) of tree swallow breeding phenology and occupancy across ~10,200 km² in southern Québec, which is directly useful for regional biodiversity monitoring, but it is single-species and of moderate temporal extent (10 years), yielding an overall moderate relevance.

High relevance: primary plot-level species cover (abundance) data and vegetation-structure measurements from 784 1-m2 plots across 67 riparian sites in southeastern Quebec (chronosequence spanning 3–17 years since planting) provide multi-species (>200) quantitative data directly applicable to Quebec biodiversity monitoring and restoration evaluation.

High relevance: the paper provides multi-year (2005–2009) GPS telemetry time-series for caribou, moose, and wolves across a large (33,900 km2) Côte‑Nord, Québec study area, including georeferenced sample points, home-range (MCP) layers, utilization distributions, habitat maps, and connectivity/network metrics; it explicitly includes a threatened species (woodland caribou) and is located in northern Quebec, making it directly applicable to Québec biodiversity monitoring.

The paper provides an author-generated, individual-level genetic dataset (nuclear microsatellites and mtDNA) sampled across 15 swarming sites including sites in Quebec (2009–2011), giving a direct genetic baseline relevant to Quebec biodiversity monitoring. Spatial coverage across multiple provinces and the pre-white-nose-syndrome baseline genetic data make it highly relevant for regional management and monitoring.

Multi-year GPS telemetry (2004–2010) for 49 threatened woodland caribou covering ~31,000 km2 in Quebec provides directly relevant movement and spatial-use data for provincial biodiversity monitoring. Temporal coverage is moderate (7 years) and spatial extent is large, and the inclusion of a threatened species supports moderate relevance for Quebec monitoring.

High relevance: produces spatially explicit habitat-raster and predicted movement maps for the endangered Atlantic‑Gaspésie caribou across ~25,000 km² of Québec, directly applicable to Quebec biodiversity monitoring and conservation planning; includes endangered-species data and scenario outputs useful for connectivity and protected-area assessments.

Authors generated primary genetic (16 microsatellite loci) and telemetry datasets and produced daily habitat-suitability and connectivity maps across Quebec and Labrador (≈1,365,000 km²). Temporal coverage is reported as daily models across an annual cycle but calendar years are not specified, so spatial and data-type relevance is high while temporal detail is incomplete — overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring assessed as moderate.

This study provides primary abundance (stem count/density) data from 19 sites within a defined 35 km2 area in eastern Quebec (collected 1997), useful for monitoring regeneration dynamics. However, its temporal coverage is a single survey (one year) and spatial extent is small, so overall relevance for Quebec-scale biodiversity monitoring is moderate.

The study provides primary datasets (physiological trait measurements, blood chemistry, PIT-tag presence/absence and passage counts/durations) for three Moxostoma species at a single fishway in Quebec across two field seasons (2011–2012). Spatial coverage is highly localized (one fishway) and temporal coverage is short (seasonal), and only three species were included, so the dataset has limited direct applicability for broad Quebec biodiversity monitoring efforts.

Primary dataset contains abundance (counts and biomass) and trait data from 108 benthic sampling stations (July 2017) — data type rates high, but temporal coverage is a single campaign and spatial extent is not quantified here. Multiple-species coverage (132 taxa) and the study location in northern Québec (Sept-Îles, >49°N) increase relevance, yielding a moderate (M) relevance for Québec biodiversity monitoring.

The paper presents primary, plot-level abundance (percent cover, biomass), species richness data and community primary productivity measurements from an experimental intertidal site in Quebec (single-season, 2011). Data type (abundance + ecosystem function) is high-value, but temporal (one season) and spatial (single site) extent are limited; multispecies sampling (>40 associated species) upgrades the relevance to moderate for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Primary data are genomic (GBS) SNPs for Arctic Char (high-value genetic data) collected at 23 sites along >2,000 km of Nunavik coastline, but the temporal extent and explicit spatial area in km2 are not stated (reducing relevance). Presence of a northern Quebec focus (bias_north_south) upgrades the relevance to moderate for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Primary dataset is directly relevant to Quebec (includes southwestern Quebec sites) and contains high-value data types (abundance counts, parasite prevalence/intensity, COI sequences, and OTU richness). However, temporal coverage is a single field season (8 weeks in 2011) and spatial extent is limited to 13 sites along a ~400 km transect, so overall relevance is moderate; the multispecies scope (>10 host species) increases its usefulness for monitoring.

Primary dataset contains abundance (count) data from repeated weekly sampling at seven named orchards in Quebec across 1999–2000 (multispecies >10 species and reports two species new to Quebec). Temporal coverage is short (2 years) and sampled spatially at small orchard blocks, so overall relevance is moderate for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Primary dataset consists of trap-level abundance counts of coprophilous and necrophagous beetles collected at four sites in central Quebec (July 2006) with species-level IDs for several taxa. Relevance is moderate: data type (abundance) and multispecies coverage (>10 species) are valuable for Quebec biodiversity monitoring, but temporal coverage is a short single-week sampling and spatial extent is limited to four nearby forest sites.

High relevance: the authors produced spatially explicit, abundance-based SDMs and species-richness maps for 37 boreal bird species across the entire province of Quebec (~1.6 million km²) using multi-decadal observations (1980–2013). These outputs (raster maps and presence/absence projections for 2050 and 2070) are directly applicable to Quebec biodiversity monitoring and planning.

Primary dataset contains multi-year (1997–1999) abundance data and species lists (73 species) from two named Quebec vineyard sites, making it directly relevant for monitoring biodiversity in Quebec. Temporal coverage is moderate (3 years) and spatial extent is small (two 0.7-ha plots), but multispecies coverage (>10 species) and several species recorded as new for Quebec increase its monitoring relevance to high.
